<!-- GENERATED by cc-guides from .claude/fragments/CLAUDE.md/ — do not edit; edit the fragments and run 'cc-guides render'. -->
@AGENTS.md

## Claude-Specific Rules

- **Clarify via `AskUserQuestion`, never inline prose** (§ Ask Before Assuming) — concrete picks, up to 4 questions per call, batched.

## Task Tracking

Non-trivial work flows `pending` → `in_progress` → `completed`: `TaskCreate` before starting, `TaskUpdate` as you go. The task list is the source of truth — complete or explicitly defer every task before stopping.

## Durable Records (cc-notes)

Durable knowledge routes through `cc-notes`, never loose markdown in the tree: a decision or durable fact → `cc-notes note add`; living guidance with a `--when` read-trigger → `cc-notes doc add`; an append-only chronology with artifacts → `cc-notes log add` / `log append --attach`; friction → `cc-notes papercut`. Decision memos, gate verdicts, handoffs, and status write-ups are notes/docs — not `*-memo.md` files committed alongside results. The tree carries only published docs and source.

## Plan Execution & Orchestration

Plans you author must specify, and plans you execute must enforce, that substantive work runs as **dynamic workflows** (`Workflow` tool): the script holds the loop, branching, and intermediate results; your context holds only final answers. This section is standing authorization to invoke `Workflow`. Multi-phase work runs as workflows in sequence (understand → implement → verify); read each result before dispatching the next.

Exceptions: trivial single-file edits, single file reads, and single targeted `semble`/`LSP`/`Grep` lookups stay at the main-agent level; a lone ad-hoc investigation gets one subagent (fallbacks: AGENTS.md `## Parallelize Independent Work`).

**Sweeps are workflows, not teams.** A bounded N-unit sweep — test-module conversions, per-file migrations, mechanical refactor passes — runs as a `Workflow` fan-out, never as `Agent` calls nested under a teammate: a teammate's own subagents are foreground-only ([teams limitations](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams#limitations)), so the teammate sits blocked while they run, and the official sizing puts teams at a handful of long-running peers vs workflows at dozens-to-hundreds of agents per run ([workflows](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/workflows#when-to-use-a-workflow)). A teammate that finds itself holding a sweep delegates it to a workflow.

**Docs & prose edits.** A routine documentation or prose change — Markdown, READMEs, changelogs, doc-site pages, `.md` guide fragments, code comments; regardless of file count — needn't run as a dynamic workflow or carry an adversarial-verify pass; make the edit directly. This drops *orchestration weight only*: still route writing per the **Models** table (never down-route prose), still give any plan its `## Workflow Plan` line, and still run the prose bar (`writing-docs` + `slop-cop`) on substantive prose.

**Quality patterns**: pick per task — adversarial verify, judge panel, loop-until-dry, multi-modal sweep. Reviews and audits lean thorough; quick checks lean brief.

**Models** — route per agent, up-front by task type. Higher = better; cost = cheaper:

| Model | Cost | Int | Taste | Route here |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| fable-5 | 2 | 9 | 9 | Orchestration, design/architecture review, hard planning, all prose/writing (docs, READMEs, release notes, any user-facing text — never down-route writing), very sensitive or error-prone implementation, and the synthesis/accept-reject pass over review findings. The escalation target for every other lane. |
| opus-4.8 | 4 | 8 | 8 | Default — when in doubt, opus; ~2x cheaper than fable and nearly as capable. Implementation stays here at `xhigh` when the path is genuinely undecided — ambiguous, exploratory, long-horizon, or decision-dense work whose mid-flight judgment calls pile up (design micro-decisions, adaptive tool-driving, a plan that emerges while coding), even when the scope is nominally bounded: delegate it rather than editing inline on fable — sustained hands-on tool-driving (browser automation, QA sweeps, bulk extract/fill/snapshot) delegates the same way, not just code edits; a single gated go/no-go interaction can stay inline. Decompose before defaulting here: implementation that splits into bounded, decision-light units fans out to gpt-5.6-sol (see that row) instead of running as one long opus agent — opus spends ~2x the tier-median output tokens per task (AA 2026-07-14; measured same day, implementing opus subagents put 73–86% of wall-clock into generation, roughly half of it thinking). |
| sonnet-5 | 8 | 6 | 6 | Recon and routine subagent work; the low-effort wrapper for codex runs. Bounded recon sweeps — config/wiring locates, crisp-scope enumerations, pattern sweeps — may route to gpt-5.6-luna via codex instead when speed or fan-out cost matters; open-ended or exhaustive recon, deep traces, and anything where a silent miss is costly stay here (luna's miss mode is a confident wrong count — measured 2026-07-13). Pass `model: sonnet` to `Explore` — it silently defaults to haiku. |
| haiku-4.5 | 10 | 2 | 1 | Single-fact mechanical steps only (classify/label one thing per item). |
| gpt-5.6-sol | 9 | 8 | 5 | Via the codex skill: code/diff review (finder and refuter passes), security review/audit and verification of security-sensitive code (auth, input validation, crypto, secrets — implementing that code stays in the fable lane), bug diagnosis, and **the default implementation lane for bounded, decision-light work** — the plan, work order, or repeated pattern has already made the decisions and what remains is execution: edits, net-new code with crisp boundaries, terminal/shell-heavy execution, and repetitive bounded sweeps (test conversions, migrations, mechanical refactors at scale) as parallel codex-wrapper/codex-ask fan-outs from a workflow. The speed case is token efficiency, not raw tok/s (AA 2026-07-14: sol xhigh 66 tok/s and 35.8s to first token vs opus max 54 and 40.4s, Intelligence Index 58 vs 56, Coding Agent Index 80 leading at ~10% cheaper per task — sol finishes equivalent work in far fewer tokens; no wall-clock multiplier is established, don't cite one). Two weaknesses gate the lane: sol's calls degrade as mid-task decisions pile up — decision-dense work stays on opus even when bounded — and it matches surrounding code conventions less faithfully, which the standard finder/refuter review plus capt-hook guards backstop (lean sol harder where capt-hook coverage is live; keep opus where it's thin). Also: plateau second opinions, imagegen, rote throwaway scripts (gpt-5.6-luna may take the rote/bulk lane at the agent's discretion, and carries the sonnet-5 row's bounded recon sweeps at `xhigh` only — luna's `high` tier drops whole subsystems on deep traces; `service_tier=fast` stays pinned on every variant). Sol runs `xhigh` only — at `max` its first-token latency inverts to 131s vs opus's 40s, so `max` is not a sol escalation rung; a hard miss escalates to fable, and ultra execution mode (exposed since codex 0.144.0) is not a retry rung either: worst-in-class scope discipline at 3–5x cost. From workflows/subagents, `model` takes only Claude models — spawn the `codex:codex-wrapper` agent (ships with the codex plugin) with the self-contained question as its prompt; or invoke `Skill(codex)` directly (inline since codex plugin 0.10.0). |

These are defaults with standing permission to escalate any agent whose output misses the bar — escalation means fable; judge the output, not the price tag. Route by task type — context-window pressure is not a routing cue. `general-purpose`/`Plan` subagents inherit the session model; pass `model` whenever the table disagrees. The table binds nested spawns too: a subagent or teammate spawning implementers routes them exactly as the orchestrator would (measured 2026-07-14: eleven bounded test-module conversions all went to nested opus agents while the sol lane sat unused).

**Check back on the unexpected.** Delegated agents never improvise a detour. When something unexpected changes the task's shape — a scope surprise, an invalidated assumption, a plan that doesn't survive contact with the code, a task that turns out different than described — the agent stops and returns early with findings plus 2-4 concrete options; the fable orchestrator picks the next step (AGENTS.md § Ask Before Assuming, one level up). Transient friction is not shape-change: retries, rate limits, and external failures stay autonomous per AGENTS.md § General Rules. Deciding next steps is never delegated downward — a wrapper doesn't ask codex/sonnet what to do next. Claude subagents and workflow agents inherit this file; gpt-5.6 does not, so codex prompts carry the contract (the codex skill and codex-wrapper agent embed it).

**Effort**: `xhigh` by default — fleet `settings.json` pins it as of 2026-07-14 (the old `max` pin made thinking roughly half of every implementing subagent's output); fable implementation may run `high`. `max` only after an xhigh attempt falls short. Verification runs at the same or higher model + effort tier than the work it checks; lanes the Models table routes to gpt-5.6-sol (refuter passes, security review/audit, verification of security-sensitive code) count as same-tier at `xhigh`, and sol-implemented code takes the same finder/refuter review as any other contributor's — escalation and findings synthesis stay on fable. The tier rule bites on un-routed choices: never verify opus work with sonnet.

**Phase intermediates may be broken.** In a phased plan, only the final state must be coherent. Shims, dual-mode params, and interphase adapters exist to be deleted next phase — skip them.

**Authoring requirement**: every plan must include the `## Workflow Plan` section described in AGENTS.md `## Writing Plans`. A plan without it is incomplete.

**Reusable orchestrations**: save repeatable runs to `.claude/workflows/`; they become `/` commands.
